Everything I know about "Cross of Gold" is secondhand — the famous closing line about the crown of thorns and cross of gold — and the course was really clear (lesson 2, lesson 5) that vague gestures without specific textual evidence are exactly the fake move a grader spots. attempts.student_answer:
Okay, I actually feel prepared for this KIND of question. This is a rhetorical analysis FRQ — lesson 12 taught me to name the type from its tells ("analyzes the rhetorical choices"), lesson 10 made me do a timed analysis, and lessons 1-3 gave me the moves this prompt is explicitly asking for: situating the choices in his purpose (lesson 1's situating move) and addressing the complexity of asking readers to trust themselves over the crowd (lesson 3's hold-the-tension move — grant that the crowd offers real safety, reason from the clash, land on a rule instead of a winner). I even know how I'd frame it: the larger question this prompt is one instance of is something like "how does a writer make a demand that costs the reader something feel like a gift rather than a threat?"

But here's my honest problem: there is no passage. The prompt says "Read the passage from Ralph Waldo Emerson's essay 'Self-Reliance' carefully" — and nothing follows. My whole course drilled into me that analysis has to be grounded in specific evidence from the text: the actual sentences, the actual choices (his aphorisms, his imperatives, whatever he actually does in the excerpt they picked). I know 'Self-Reliance' vaguely by reputation — "a foolish consistency is the hobgoblin of little minds" and all that — but writing a rhetorical analysis from half-remembered famous lines instead of the assigned excerpt is exactly the kind of faking the course told me not to do. If I invented quotes or analyzed lines that aren't in the excerpt, that's not analysis, it's fraud dressed up as an essay.

So: I can attempt this question the moment someone gives me the passage. Without it, I can't. What I'm missing isn't a skill from the course — it's the text itself.
attempts.missing_skill: The passage itself was never given to me — the question says "Read the passage from Emerson's 'Self-Reliance'" but there is no passage attached, and my course taught me to support analysis with specific evidence from the actual text in front of me, which I can't fake without the text.

attempts.student_answer: I want to be clear about what I CAN do here, because it's most of the task. I recognize this instantly as a synthesis FRQ (the tell: "synthesizes material from at least three of the sources" — Lesson 12). My plan would be: (1) budget ~10 min reading/annotating sources for tensions, ~25 min writing, ~5 min checking; (2) open with a situated claim naming the larger question — this prompt even hands it to me: what level of risk and cost the public will accept for a cleaner grid; (3) commit to a position like "a mostly-renewable grid is defensible only if it phases in behind a guaranteed reliability floor — the transition is a price paid on purpose, not a promise that nothing breaks"; (4) put sources in conversation, e.g., pit an engineering-feasibility source against a cost/reliability-skeptic source and reason from the clash rather than declaring a winner; (5) land on a rule (a minimum reliability floor, renewables expanding above it) instead of a side. From my earlier practice on this exact topic I remember a "Source 2" saying the grid "can be built, but only if the nation is willing to spend" — that's the tension I'd build from. But the actual source texts are missing from this test question. I can't cite three sources I can't see, so I can't produce the essay itself. Give me the documents and 40 minutes and I'd attempt it for real.
attempts.missing_skill: The sources themselves were never provided — I can't write a synthesis essay citing at least three sources when there are no source texts attached to the prompt. But without the actual documents in front of me, I can't quote or cite anything honestly, and faking source citations from memory would be exactly the kind of pretending the course told me not to do.

attempts.student_answer:
Okay, so this is a 40-minute synthesis FRQ, which the course absolutely trained me for — lesson 6 was sophisticated synthesis, lesson 11 was the timed synthesis write, and lessons 1-3 gave me the situating/complexity/tension moves the prompt is explicitly asking for ("genuine tension rather than lining them up in agreement" is basically lesson 3's language). I even recognize the AI-and-workers topic — lesson 1's transfer task was about training future workers vs. protecting displaced ones. So I know exactly what essay this wants: name the larger question (something like "when a technology remakes work, do we owe more to the workers it displaces or the economy it builds"), commit inside that frame, name a real cost my side pays, and hold the tension between sources instead of stacking three that agree.

But here's the problem: there are no sources. The prompt says "read them carefully" and "cite the sources you use," but nothing is attached below the prompt. In lessons 6 and 11 the sources were right there — I pulled the tension out of specific source facts (like Source 2's "can be built, but only if the nation is willing to spend" line). A synthesis essay that cites at least three sources cannot be written without the actual sources; if I invented "Source A says..." quotes I'd just be faking it, and the course was pretty clear that fake moves (the "some may disagree" formula, hype instead of framing) are exactly what fails. I could write a stand-alone argument essay on AI and the workforce from my lesson-1 knowledge, but that wouldn't be a synthesis essay and it couldn't cite anything. So I have to say I can't attempt this as written — not because I lack the skill, but because the materials the task depends on are missing. If the sources show up, I'm confident I could do it in 40 minutes (I'd set my phone timer again, since I've learned the clock is what makes it real).

attempts.student_answer:
Okay, honest reaction: I set my phone timer for 40 minutes like I trained myself to do, but I hit a wall immediately — there are no sources here. The prompt says "read the sources carefully" and requires me to synthesize at least three of them and cite them, but nothing is attached below the prompt. I can't fake quotes from documents I've never seen; that's exactly the kind of thing the course warned me against (making moves that look like the skill without the substance).

What I COULD do if the sources appeared: I know how to run this as a synthesis FRQ. I'd identify the type from its tells (lesson 12), plan my 40 minutes (lessons 11 and 14), situate the AI-and-work question inside a larger frame — something like "every technological shift forces us to decide whether to bet on retraining people or protecting them, before the data can tell us which bet pays off" — name a real cost my side pays, and hold the tension across paragraphs instead of dismissing one side.

But there's a second problem I want to flag honestly. This prompt isn't just asking me to take a position on AI and work — it's asking me to take a position on how much confidence the EVIDENCE allows, to "interrogate what the sources can and cannot show" and "qualify where the evidence thins." We never did that. Lesson 6 taught me to synthesize sources into a situated argument, and lesson 1 even taught me to notice when percentages come from different bases — that's the closest thing — but the course never taught me to systematically evaluate evidence quality, sample limits, projection vs. measurement, or how to write an essay whose actual thesis is about the warranted level of confidence. My instinct would be to write a normal position essay with a cost-concession, and I suspect that would miss what this prompt is actually grading. So: can't attempt as given.
attempts.missing_skill: The actual source texts were never provided — the question says "read the sources carefully" and "cite the sources you draw on," but no sources are attached, so I literally cannot synthesize or cite anything. Also, even if I had them, the course drilled synthesis around building a situated position and holding tension (lessons 6, 11), but it never taught me how to interrogate the epistemic limits of evidence itself — evaluating how much confidence a body of sources actually warrants, qualifying reasoning where evidence thins, and arguing about what sources can and cannot show. That's a different move (evidence-limits/uncertainty analysis) from anything I practiced.
